moveAnnotations.annotation: We start with an unusual first move that immediately develops a piece and supports the center. The Van Geet Opening prioritizes piece activity over classical center control, and Nc3 lets us support a future e4 push while keeping our options flexible. This move prepares to challenge Black's setup from an unexpected angle—most players expect 1.e4 or 1.d4, so you're immediately playing outside normal patterns. By developing first, we invite Black to stake a claim in the center, which we'll then attack.

Black takes space in the center immediately, which is the natural response to our modest first move. They're claiming d5 before we can occupy it and preparing to develop pieces on solid ground. This aggressive continuation is why Black can be dangerous in the Van Geet—you need to have concrete plans, not just hope for a slow maneuvering game. We now have the opportunity to execute the Dunst-Perrenet Gambit by striking at their center with e4.

Now we attack Black's pawn and offer a gambit—we're willing to sacrifice our e-pawn to disrupt their center and open lines for our pieces. By playing e4 after they've committed to d5, we force them to make an immediate decision: take our pawn or retreat. The beauty of this gambit is that whether they capture or retreat, we gain time and initiative because our Nc3 is already developed and supporting the center. This aggressive middle-ground strategy avoids the heavy theory of mainline openings.

Black accepts the gambit, which is the critical and most forcing continuation. By capturing on e4, they remove our dangerous attacking pawn but give us back our time with tempo. Now our pieces are positioned to create threats quickly—the Nc3 supports our center, and we have d3 coming to reinforce the position. This is actually what we wanted, because we're not trying to regain the pawn immediately; we're trying to generate active piece play and keep Black under pressure.

We recapture on d3, solidifying our center and preparing to bring more pieces into the attack. The move d3 supports e4 indirectly and prepares f4 if we want to push further, while also opening the diagonal for our light-squared bishop. We're not worried about being a pawn down yet because our piece coordination is strong: Nc3 is active, and our next moves (f4, Qe2, or Nf3) will create immediate tactical threats. The gambit has succeeded in creating an imbalanced position where you have the initiative and Black must prove they can handle the pressure.
